title: A Warning Is Not a Decision
context: An operating-model review has been called for phase-one expense policy enforcement. In the room are the Finance Controller, Product Manager, Payroll Lead, Operations Director, and Engineering Manager. Finance wants stronger control before quarter close. Operations wants managers to keep discretion for edge cases. Engineering wants a design that ships cleanly without turning a focused workflow into a platform rebuild. You are expected to frame the decision in the room.

questions.prompt: The Finance Controller says: "Any claim outside policy should be blocked automatically. If we allow submission, people will assume the rule does not matter." How do you respond?

options.text: That is the cleanest control model, so we should make every policy breach a hard stop. It gives Finance consistency, removes manager subjectivity, and keeps the product message simple for employees from the first release onward. It also gives Finance a stronger control message and postpones the harder operating-model trade-offs until the workflow is already live.
options.explanation: The desire for consistency is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it jumps straight to the heaviest control outcome without first separating advisory rules from decision rules.

options.text: I would separate warning policies from decision policies before we choose the mechanism. Some breaches may justify a hard stop, but others belong as auditable warnings with manager review. The right split depends on ownership, audit need, and the cost of false negatives versus false positives.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the framing pattern and the 5 decision criteria — volatility, ownership, audit, latency, and reuse. The answer separates the problem before choosing technology and anchors the room on ownership and audit rather than treating every rule as the same kind of control.

options.text: Blocking everything would create too much friction, so I would avoid it. Managers need room to use judgment, and Finance will have to accept that some policy breaches are part of business reality.
options.explanation: The concern about user friction is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it pushes back on the hard-stop idea without offering a structured way to decide which policies deserve which treatment.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Manager adds: "If exceptions need routing, we should stand up a workflow engine now so policy breaches can take different paths later." What do you say?

options.text: Yes, we should establish the engine now. Once policy breaches exist, different paths, escalations, and timers will follow, so it is more efficient to invest in the general platform than to revisit workflow architecture after launch. It also gives Finance a stronger control message and postpones the harder operating-model trade-offs until the workflow is already live.
options.explanation: The wish to avoid rework is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it uses a plausible future need to justify a heavier platform decision before the current workflow shape has been bounded.

options.text: For phase one, I would keep the design tied to the actual approval problem in front of us. If we only need status changes, manager review, and clear audit of who approved an exception, that is not yet evidence for a workflow platform decision. We can name the carve-out criteria now and revisit only if the process becomes materially more stateful.
options.explanation: This demonstrates naming the simpler option and forcing the heavier one to be justified. The answer keeps the room on today's workflow shape and sets criteria for when a platform step-up would be warranted.

options.text: Let us do a technical spike on workflow products and compare several target-state designs before we answer. Once we have that analysis, we can decide whether the exceptions warrant an engine or a lighter path.
options.explanation: The urge to gather options is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect already has enough to frame the decision by distinguishing a bounded approval flow from a workflow platform choice.

questions.prompt: The Payroll Lead asks: "Employees with multiple assignments often create ambiguity over which cost centre should absorb an out-of-policy claim. Can we infer the most likely one to keep the flow moving?" How do you respond?

options.text: We should infer it automatically from the employee's primary assignment and only correct it later if someone spots a problem. That keeps the submission flow smooth and avoids asking employees to make finance decisions they may not understand. It also gives Finance a stronger control message and postpones the harder operating-model trade-offs until the workflow is already live.
options.explanation: The desire to reduce effort is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it turns an ambiguous finance choice into silent system behaviour without testing ownership or audit implications.

options.text: I would avoid hidden inference where the business decision is materially ambiguous. If cost attribution matters for approval, reporting, or payroll treatment, the workflow should surface the choice explicitly or route it to the right approver rather than pretend the ambiguity is not there.
options.explanation: This demonstrates using ownership and audit criteria in the room. The answer recognises that not every convenience is a safe automation and keeps accountability explicit when the choice has business consequences.

options.text: That sounds messy enough that we should postpone multi-assignment handling to a later release. Phase one will be cleaner if the feature only works for employees with one assignment and everyone else stays manual for now.
options.explanation: The instinct to protect scope is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: it gives up control of an important boundary when the architect can still frame a principled answer without redesigning the whole release.

questions.prompt: Operations says: "If a manager rejects a claim because it breaches policy, we should convert that into a request for changes so the employee can edit and resubmit without creating another record." What is your response?

options.text: That is a cleaner experience, so we should redefine rejection that way everywhere. Employees will see a smoother path, and we avoid multiple records for what is effectively the same claim journey.
options.explanation: The product instinct is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it prioritises flow neatness before checking whether the business semantics of reject and revise are actually the same.

options.text: I would keep the business outcomes explicit. If 'rejected' means the decision was no, we should preserve that audit state and provide a deliberate amend-and-resubmit path if needed. We should not relabel a business decision only because it feels tidier in the UI.
options.explanation: This demonstrates protecting business semantics and closing off hidden coupling. The answer keeps audit meaning intact and avoids redesigning the workflow vocabulary around a convenience shortcut.

options.text: We should probably take that away and map every outcome state before deciding. Once we have a full lifecycle diagram, we can come back and agree which labels belong in phase one.
options.explanation: The desire for consistency is reasonable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the room already has enough information to decide that semantic clarity matters more than collapsing states for convenience.

questions.prompt: The group looks to you to close. Which summary is strongest?

options.text: We have agreed to classify policy checks before automating them, keep phase one on a bounded approval flow rather than a workflow platform, avoid silent inference where cost ownership is ambiguous, and preserve clear business outcomes when managers decide. Product and Operations can now design the journey inside those constraints.
options.explanation: This demonstrates meeting closure and disciplined framing. The summary captures the decisions, the criteria behind them, and the bounded next step without reopening the debate.

options.text: There are enough open questions that we should avoid locking the design today. Each team can return with more examples of policy breaches, and we can decide the automation level once we have broader evidence.
options.explanation: The caution is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: it abandons the architect's job of framing the direction when the room already has enough to set decision boundaries.

options.text: To stay future-proof, we should design for blocking, exception routing, inferred cost allocation, and editable resubmission from the start so that no policy pattern is excluded later. That gives us one unified control model.
options.explanation: The wish for completeness is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it bundles multiple heavier choices together in the name of future-proofing and increases delivery risk without proof they are all needed.
